239:     style Core fill:#fff3e0
240: ```
241: 
242: **Event Types**:
243: - `agent_event`: Agent messages and thinking process
244: - `agent_streaming`: Token-by-token LLM output
245: - `tool_call`: Tool invocation with parameters
246: - `tool_result`: Tool execution results
247: - `progress`: Stage completion percentages
248: - `input_request`: HITL confirmation requests
249: - `project_log`: External process stdout/stderr
250: 
251: **Process Runner Architecture**:
252: - **Cross-platform**: Windows (`cmd /C`) vs Unix (`sh -c`) command execution
253: - **Process Registry**: `Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>` tracks active processes by iteration_id
254: - **Stream Processing**: Separate Tokio tasks read stdout/stderr asynchronously
255: - **Safety**: `is_blocking_service_command()` prevents execution of hanging commands (npm dev, http.server)
256: 
257: ### 2.5 Memory Query and Knowledge Retrieval Flow
258: 
259: **Business Value**: Maintains project continuity across iterations by preserving architectural decisions, design patterns, and technical insights.
260: 
261: ```mermaid
262: flowchart LR
263:     A[Query Request] --> B{Query Scope}
264:     
265:     B -->|Project| C[ProjectMemory<br/>Decisions & Patterns]
266:     B -->|Iteration| D[IterationKnowledge<br/>Specific Insights]
267:     B -->|Smart| E[Merged View<br/>Context Aggregation]
268:     
269:     C --> F[MemoryStore Query]
270:     D --> F
271:     E --> F
272:     
273:     F --> G[Fuzzy Keyword Search]
274:     G --> H[Filter by Category]
275:     H --> I[Generate Context String]
276:     I --> J[LLM Consumption]
277:     
278:     style A fill:#e1f5fe
279:     style J fill:#c8e6c9
280: ```
281: 
282: **Query Dimensions**:
283: 1. **Scope**: `project` (historical), `iteration` (current), `latest` (merged)
284: 2. **Category**: `decisions`, `patterns`, `insights`, `issues`
285: 3. **Stage**: Filter by pipeline stage (idea, prd, design, etc.)
286: 4. **Keywords**: Fuzzy search on titles and content
287: 
288: **Knowledge Promotion Workflow**:
289: - **Insight Capture**: `SaveInsightTool` captures observations during execution
290: - **Decision Promotion**: `PromoteToDecisionTool` elevates insights to project-level architectural decisions
291: - **Pattern Extraction**: `PromoteToPatternTool` creates reusable pattern library entries
292: - **Snapshot Generation**: Post-delivery, `SaveKnowledgeSnapshotTool` compresses iteration data into `IterationKnowledge`
293: 
294: ## 3. Flow Coordination and Control
295: 
296: ### 3.1 State Management Architecture
297: 
298: **Pipeline State Machine**:
299: 
300: ```mermaid
301: stateDiagram-v2
302:     [*] --> Draft: Create Iteration
303:     Draft --> Running: Start Execution
304:     Running --> Paused: User Pause / HITL Wait
305:     Paused --> Running: Continue / Retry
306:     Running --> Completed: All Stages Success
307:     Running --> Failed: Error / Exception
308:     
309:     Completed --> [*]
310:     Failed --> Running: Retry with Feedback
311:     Failed --> [*]: Abort
312: ```
313: 
314: **Context Propagation**:
315: - `PipelineContext` carries execution state across stages containing:
316:   - Current iteration ID and metadata
317:   - Stage sequence and completion tracking
318:   - Inheritance mode and base knowledge references
319:   - User feedback accumulation
320: - **Immutable Updates**: Each stage receives cloned context, outputs new context with updated artifacts
321: 
322: ### 3.2 Multi-Module Coordination
323: 
324: **Dependency Injection Pattern**:
325: ```rust
326: // Pipeline orchestrates through trait abstractions
327: pub struct Pipeline {
328:     stages: Vec<Box<dyn Stage>>,
329:     backend: Arc<dyn InteractiveBackend>,
330:     store: Arc<IterationStore>,
331: }
332: ```
333: 
334: **Concurrency Control**:
335: - **Single Iteration Execution**: Global semaphore in `RateLimitedLlm` ensures one active LLM request at a time
336: - **GUI State Sharing**: `AppState` wrapped in `Arc<Mutex<>>` for thread-safe access across Tauri commands
337: - **Process Isolation**: Each iteration's dev server runs in isolated subprocess with dedicated I/O streams
338: 
339: ### 3.3 Data Flow Architecture
340: 
341: **Artifact Flow**:
342: 1. **Generation**: AI Actor creates content via LLM inference
343: 2. **Validation**: AI Critic checks quality standards
344: 3. **Review**: HITL gate presents to user via InteractiveBackend
345: 4. **Persistence**: Saved to `workspace/artifacts/{stage}.md`
346: 5. **Propagation**: Loaded by subsequent stages via `LoadDocumentSummaryTool`
347: 
348: **Memory Flow**:
349: 1. **Capture**: Agent tools (`SaveInsightTool`, `SaveIssueTool`) collect knowledge
350: 2. **Aggregation**: `MemoryStore` indexes by tags and categories
351: 3. **Query**: `QueryMemoryTool` retrieves relevant context
352: 4. **Injection**: Context strings prepended to agent prompts for continuity
353: 
354: ## 4. Exception Handling and Recovery
355: 
356: ### 4.1 Error Classification Hierarchy
357: 
358: | Level | Type | Handling Strategy |
359: |-------|------|-------------------|
360: | **Infrastructure** | LLM API failures, network timeouts | Exponential backoff, rate limit respect, user notification |
361: | **Domain** | Invalid state transitions, constraint violations | Validation at entry points, early failure with context |
362: | **Tool** | File I/O errors, command execution failures | Path validation, sandbox enforcement, detailed error messages |
363: | **Pipeline** | Stage execution failures, agent errors | Pause iteration, allow retry with feedback, preserve partial artifacts |
364: 
365: ### 4.2 Recovery Mechanisms
366: 
367: **Iteration State Recovery**:
368: - **Orphaned State Detection**: On project load, automatically reset `Running` iterations to `Paused` (handles crashes)
369: - **Partial Artifact Recovery**: Stage completion tracked separately; can resume from any completed stage
370: - **Workspace Integrity**: Atomic file operations with validation before overwriting
371: 
372: **HITL Timeout Handling**:
373: - **GUI Mode**: 3000-second timeout on oneshot channels; auto-fail iteration if user unresponsive
374: - **CLI Mode**: Blocking wait with `ctrl+c` handling for graceful interruption
375: 
376: **Tool Execution Safety**:
377: - **Path Validation**: `validate_path_security_within_workspace()` prevents directory traversal (`..` rejection, absolute path rejection)
378: - **Command Blocking**: Detection of long-running service commands (npm dev, vite) to prevent agent hangs
379: - **File Extension Filtering**: Deployment tool uses whitelist approach (`.html`, `.js`, `.css`, etc.) preventing sensitive file exposure
380: 
381: ### 4.3 Fault Tolerance Strategies
382: 
383: **Graceful Degradation**:
384: - **LLM Unavailable**: Pipeline pauses with clear error message; user can retry
385: - **Editor Unavailable**: HITL falls back to text input mode if external editor launch fails
386: - **Process Crash**: `project_runner` emits `project_stopped` event; GUI updates UI state
387: 
388: **Retry Logic**:
389: - **Transient Failures**: LLM rate limiting (429 errors) trigger automatic retry with delay
390: - **Validation Failures**: Actor-Critic loop allows up to 3 automatic regeneration attempts before human intervention required
391: 
392: ## 5. Key Process Implementation
393: 
394: ### 5.1 Stage Execution Algorithm
395: 
396: ```rust
397: // Pseudocode representation of stage execution logic
398: async fn execute_stage(
399:     &self,
400:     context: PipelineContext,
401:     backend: Arc<dyn InteractiveBackend>
402: ) -> StageResult {
403:     // 1. Load stage-specific instructions (Actor + Critic)
404:     let actor_prompt = self.get_actor_instruction();
405:     let critic_prompt = self.get_critic_instruction();
406:     
407:     // 2. Create ADK agent with tool registry
408:     let agent = AgentBuilder::new()
409:         .with_llm(create_llm_client())
410:         .with_tools(get_stage_tools())
411:         .with_instruction(actor_prompt)
412:         .build();
413:     
414:     // 3. Execute with streaming output
415:     let mut stream = agent.execute(context.to_prompt()).await;
416:     while let Some(chunk) = stream.next().await {
417:         backend.show_message(chunk.content).await;
418:         
419:         // Handle tool calls
420:         if let Some(tool_call) = chunk.tool_call {
421:             backend.show_tool_call(&tool_call).await;
422:             let result = execute_tool(tool_call).await;
423:             backend.show_tool_result(&result).await;
424:             agent.submit_tool_result(result).await;
425:         }
426:     }
427:     
428:     // 4. Critic validation
429:     if let Some(critic_result) = validate_with_critic(&agent.output).await {
430:         if !critic_result.approved {
431:             return StageResult::Revision(critic_result.feedback);
432:         }
433:     }
434:     
435:     // 5. HITL confirmation (if required)
436:     if self.needs_confirmation() {
437:         match backend.request_confirmation(&agent.output).await {
438:             UserAction::Pass => StageResult::Success(agent.output),
439:             UserAction::Edit => {
440:                 let edited = open_editor(&agent.output).await;
441:                 StageResult::Success(edited)
442:             }
443:             UserAction::Feedback(fb) => StageResult::Revision(fb),
444:         }
445:     } else {
446:         StageResult::Success(agent.output)
447:     }
448: }
449: ```
450: 
451: ### 5.2 Knowledge Extraction Pipeline
452: 
453: **Post-Delivery Processing**:
454: 1. **Document Analysis**: Parse idea.md, prd.md, design.md, plan.md for key themes
455: 2. **Tech Stack Detection**: Analyze configuration files (package.json, Cargo.toml, requirements.txt)
456: 3. **Code Structure Mapping**: Directory traversal with depth limiting (max 10 levels)
457: 4. **Decision Extraction**: Identify architectural choices with rationale and consequences
458: 5. **Pattern Recognition**: Extract reusable solutions with usage examples
459: 6. **Issue Documentation**: Capture known problems and technical debt
460: 
461: **Storage Format**:
462: ```json
463: {
464:   "iteration_id": "uuid",
465:   "timestamp": "ISO 8601",
466:   "summary": "300-500 word narrative",
467:   "tech_stack": ["React", "Rust", "TypeScript"],
468:   "decisions": [{"title": "", "rationale": "", "consequences": []}],
469:   "patterns": [{"name": "", "tags": [], "usage": ""}],
470:   "known_issues": [{"description": "", "severity": "high|medium|low"}]
471: }
472: ```
473: 
474: ### 5.3 Project Manager Agent Interaction Flow
475: 
476: **Trigger Condition**: After iteration status becomes `Completed` (after Delivery stage), GUI automatically switches to PM Agent chat mode.
477: 
478: **Interaction Pattern**:
479: ```
480: Iteration Completed → PM Agent Enabled → User Natural Language Interaction → Agent Intent Analysis → Execute Operation
481: ```
482: 
483: **Supported Operations**:
484: 1. **Stage Return** (goto_stage): Return to previous stages for re-execution
485:    - Available stages: idea, prd, design, plan, coding
486:    - Requires user confirmation before execution
487: 
488: 2. **Create New Iteration** (create_iteration): Create new iteration based on new requirements
489:    - Automatically jumps to plan stage to start execution
490: 
491: 3. **Q&A Interaction**: Answer questions about the project
492:    - Provides context information (artifacts, decisions, memory)
493: 
494: **Language Preference**: System automatically reads Windows system language setting, Agent generates content using corresponding language:
495: - Chinese (zh-CN): Simplified Chinese response
496: - Japanese (ja): 日本語 response
497: - English (en): English response
498: - Other: Default English
499: 
500: **Technical Implementation**:
501: - Frontend: `pmMessages[]` state manages chat history, `chatMode` auto-switches
502: - Backend: `pm_send_message` command handles messages, `execute_pm_agent_message` executes Agent
503: - Global config: `get_system_locale()` gets system language, `get_language_instruction()` generates language instruction
504: 
505: ### 5.3 File System Security Model
506: 
507: **Validation Pipeline**:
508: ```mermaid
509: flowchart TD
510:     A[Tool receives path] --> B{Is Absolute?}
511:     B -->|Yes| C[Reject: Security Violation]
512:     B -->|No| D{Contains .. ?}
513:     D -->|Yes| C
514:     D -->|No| E[Resolve to absolute]
515:     E --> F{Within workspace?}
516:     F -->|No| C
517:     F -->|Yes| G[Strip UNC prefix if Windows]
518:     G --> H[Execute operation]
519:     
520:     style C fill:#ffcdd2
521:     style H fill:#c8e6c9
522: ```
523: 
524: **Protected Paths** (Deployment Phase):
525: - Version control: `.git`, `.gitignore`, `.gitattributes`
526: - IDE config: `.vscode`, `.idea`
527: - Build artifacts: `target`, `node_modules`, `dist`, `build`
528: - System files: `.cowork-v2`, `Cargo.lock` (optional)
529: - Documentation: `README.md`, `LICENSE`, `CHANGELOG.md`
530: 
531: ### 5.4 Performance Optimization Strategies
532: 
533: **LLM Rate Limiting**:
534: - **Global Semaphore**: `tokio::sync::Semaphore(1)` ensures single concurrent request
535: - **Request Spacing**: 2-second delay between requests enforces 30 req/min policy
536: - **Token Streaming**: Real-time token emission prevents UI blocking during long generations
537: 
538: **File I/O Optimization**:
539: - **Truncated Reading**: `ReadFileTruncatedTool` limits large files (>10KB) to prevent context overflow
540: - **Virtualized Rendering**: GUI file tree uses `react-window` for handling directories with 1000+ files
541: - **Chunked Loading**: Large files loaded in 10KB chunks with offset/limit pagination
542: 
543: **Memory Management**:
544: - **Lazy Loading**: Memory index loaded on first query, cached for subsequent requests
545: - **Context Pruning**: LLM prompts include only top-N relevant memories (configurable limit)
546: - **Workspace Cleanup**: Automatic removal of orphaned files during deployment phase
547: 
548: ---
549: 
550: **End of Core Workflows Documentation**
````

### litho.docs/en/4.Deep-Exploration/Interaction Domain.md (330 lines)

````
1: **Interaction Domain Technical Documentation**
2: **Cowork Forge System**
3: *Generation Time: 2024-12-19*
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: ## 1. Executive Summary
8: 
9: The **Interaction Domain** serves as the infrastructure bridge between Cowork Forge's core business logic and its user interfaces. Following the Hexagonal Architecture pattern (Ports and Adapters), this domain implements the `InteractiveBackend` trait—a standardized port that enables the system's dual-interface strategy. 
10: 
11: By abstracting user interaction behind a unified asynchronous interface, the domain allows the AI pipeline (7-stage development workflow) to execute identically across both automation-focused CLI environments and interactive desktop GUI experiences without modification to core domain logic.
12: 
13: **Key Capabilities:**
14: - **Dual-Interface Abstraction**: Single trait implementation supporting both terminal (CLI) and desktop (Tauri) interfaces
15: - **Real-time Streaming**: Low-latency transmission of AI agent outputs and tool execution results
16: - **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)**: Synchronous request-response coordination for human validation gates
17: - **Context-Aware Messaging**: Rich metadata propagation enabling sophisticated UI rendering
18: 
19: ---
20: 
21: ## 2. Architectural Positioning
22: 
23: ### 2.1 Layer Classification
24: In the Domain-Driven Design (DDD) architecture, the Interaction Domain resides in the **Infrastructure Layer**, implementing outbound adapters for the presentation layer:
25: 
26: ```mermaid
27: flowchart TB
28:     subgraph Presentation[Presentation Layer]
29:         CLI[cowork-cli]
30:         GUI[cowork-gui<br/>React + Tauri]
31:     end
32:     
33:     subgraph Application[Application Layer]
34:         Pipeline[Pipeline Executor]
35:         Tools[Tool System]
36:     end
37:     
38:     subgraph Infrastructure[Infrastructure Layer]
39:         Interaction[Interaction Domain<br/>InteractiveBackend Trait]
40:         LLM[LLM Integration]
41:         Persistence[Persistence]
42:     end
43:     
44:     CLI -->|implements| Interaction
45:     GUI -->|implements| Interaction
46:     Pipeline -->|uses| Interaction
47:     Tools -->|uses| Interaction
48: ```
49: 
50: ### 2.2 Design Pattern: Port and Adapter
51: The domain implements the **Port and Adapter** pattern through:
52: - **Port**: `InteractiveBackend` trait defining the contract for all UI interactions
53: - **Adapters**: Concrete implementations (`CliBackend`, `TauriBackend`) adapting the port to specific technologies
54: 
55: This decoupling ensures that changes to the React frontend or terminal libraries never propagate to the Pipeline or Tool domains.
56: 
57: ---
58: 
59: ## 3. Core Abstraction: InteractiveBackend Trait
60: 
61: The `InteractiveBackend` trait defines the contract for bidirectional communication between the AI pipeline and users. It is implemented using Rust's `async_trait` macro to support asynchronous operations across the tokio runtime.
62: 
63: ### 3.1 Interface Definition
64: 
65: | Method | Purpose | Direction |
66: |--------|---------|-----------|
67: | `show_message` | Display system notifications (Info/Warning/Error/Success) | Backend → User |
68: | `show_message_with_context` | Rich messaging with agent/stage metadata | Backend → User |
69: | `send_streaming` | Real-time LLM token streaming | Backend → User |
70: | `send_tool_call` | Tool invocation visualization | Backend → User |
71: | `send_tool_result` | Tool execution completion display | Backend → User |
72: | `show_progress` | Task progress reporting (0-100%) | Backend → User |
73: | `request_input` | HITL input collection with timeout | Backend ↔ User |
74: | `submit_response` | Async response handling for pending requests | User → Backend |
75: 
76: ### 3.2 Context-Aware Messaging
77: The `MessageContext` struct enables sophisticated UI rendering by carrying metadata:
78: 
79: ```rust
80: struct MessageContext {
81:     agent_name: String,      // e.g., "Actor", "Critic"
82:     stage_name: String,      // e.g., "Design", "Coding"
83:     message_type: MessageType // Normal, Thinking, ToolCall, ToolResult, Streaming
84: }
85: ```
86: 
87: This context allows the GUI to apply color coding, agent avatars, and stage-specific grouping without parsing message content.
88: 
89: ---
90: 
91: ## 4. Implementation: CLI Backend
92: 
93: Located in `crates/cowork-core/src/interaction/cli.rs`, the CLI implementation provides terminal-based interaction using the `dialoguer` and `console` crates.
94: 
95: ### 4.1 Technical Characteristics
96: - **Stateless Design**: No persistent state between method calls
97: - **UTF-8 Compliance**: Full Unicode support for international project names and content
98: - **External Editor Integration**: Uses `dialoguer::Editor` to launch system default editors (VS Code, Vim, etc.) for content review
99: - **Colored Output**: Emoji-prefixed, ANSI-colored messages using the `console` crate
100: 
101: ### 4.2 HITL Implementation
102: For Human-in-the-Loop interactions, the CLI backend:
103: 1. Displays content preview with syntax highlighting hints
104: 2. Presents options: `[Pass]`, `[Edit]`, `[Cancel]`, or `[Feedback]`
105: 3. For "Edit" selection: spawns external editor process and monitors file changes
106: 4. Returns structured `InputResponse` to the awaiting pipeline stage
107: 
108: ### 4.3 Streaming Behavior
109: Real-time LLM streaming is implemented via `stdout` flushing:
110: ```rust
111: print!("{}", chunk);
112: std::io::stdout().flush().unwrap();
113: ```
114: This ensures tokens appear immediately rather than buffering until newline characters.
115: 
116: ---
117: 
118: ## 5. Implementation: Tauri Backend
119: 
120: Located in `crates/cowork-core/src/interaction/tauri.rs`, the Tauri implementation enables desktop GUI interaction through event emission and command invocation.
121: 
122: ### 5.1 Event-Driven Architecture
123: The backend uses Tauri's `AppHandle` to emit events to the WebView frontend:
124: 
125: | Event Name | Payload | Purpose |
126: |------------|---------|---------|
127: | `agent_event` | Structured message with context | General agent communication |
128: | `agent_streaming` | Token chunk + metadata | Real-time LLM output |
129: | `tool_call` | Tool name + arguments | Tool execution start |
130: | `tool_result` | Result data + success flag | Tool execution completion |
131: | `progress` | Task ID + percentage | Long-running task updates |
132: | `input_request` | Request ID + prompt + options | HITL modal trigger |
133: | `project_loaded` | Project metadata | State change notification |
134: 
135: ### 5.2 Synchronous HITL in Async Context
136: The most complex aspect of the Tauri backend is managing synchronous user input within the async pipeline. The implementation uses **tokio oneshot channels**:
137: 
138: ```mermaid
139: sequenceDiagram
140:     participant Agent as AI Agent
141:     participant Backend as TauriBackend
142:     participant State as AppState (Mutex)
143:     participant Frontend as React Frontend
144:     participant User as User
145:     
146:     Agent->>Backend: request_input(prompt, options)
147:     Backend->>State: Generate request_id
148:     Backend->>State: Store oneshot::Sender in pending_requests HashMap
149:     Backend->>Frontend: emit("input_request", request_id, ...)
150:     Backend->>Backend: rx.await with 3000s timeout
151:     
152:     Frontend->>User: Display input modal
153:     User->>Frontend: Submit response
154:     Frontend->>Backend: invoke("submit_input_response", request_id, ...)
155:     Backend->>State: Lookup and remove request_id
156:     Backend->>Backend: tx.send(InputResponse)
157:     Backend-->>Agent: Return InputResponse
158: ```
159: 
160: **Key Implementation Details:**
161: - **Pending Requests Storage**: `HashMap<String, oneshot::Sender<InputResponse>>` protected by `Arc<Mutex<>>`
162: - **Timeout Handling**: 3000-second (50-minute) timeout using `tokio::select!` to prevent indefinite pipeline stalls
163: - **Request Correlation**: UUID-based request IDs ensure responses match to the correct awaiting agent
164: 
165: ### 5.3 State Management (V2 Architecture)
166: The V2 architecture removed the event bus in favor of direct state sharing:
167: ```rust
168: pub struct AppState {
169:     pending_requests: HashMap<String, oneshot::Sender<InputResponse>>,
170:     project_registry_manager: ProjectRegistryManager,
171:     workspace_path: Option<PathBuf>,
172: }
173: ```
174: Access is synchronized via `Arc<Mutex<AppState>>` shared across Tauri commands and the Interaction backend.
175: 
176: ---
177: 
178: ## 6. Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) Mechanism
179: 
180: The HITL system enables the Actor-Critic pattern by pausing AI execution for human validation at critical stage boundaries.
181: 
182: ### 6.1 Interaction Flow
183: 
184: **Step 1: Trigger**
185: - Stage Executor detects `needs_confirmation()` or agent calls `ReviewAndEditContentTool`
186: - Tool retrieves `InteractiveBackend` from pipeline context
187: 
188: **Step 2: Presentation**
189: - **CLI**: Renders content preview in terminal with dialoguer prompts
190: - **GUI**: Emits `input_request` event; React frontend displays modal dialog
191: 
192: **Step 3: User Action**
193: - **Pass**: Approve and continue pipeline
194: - **Edit**: Modify content in external editor (CLI) or inline editor (GUI)
195: - **Feedback**: Provide comments triggering agent regeneration via `execute_with_feedback()`
196: 
197: **Step 4: Resolution**
198: - CLI receives input directly via stdin
199: - GUI receives input via `submit_input_response` Tauri command, which resolves the oneshot channel
200: 
201: ### 6.2 Error Handling
202: - **Timeout**: Returns `InputResponse::Timeout` after 3000s, allowing pipeline to abort or continue based on configuration
203: - **Cancellation**: User can cancel iteration, triggering pipeline abortion and cleanup
204: - **Connection Loss**: GUI disconnections are handled by timeout mechanisms; CLI handles Ctrl+C via signal handlers
205: 
206: ---
207: 
208: ## 7. Integration Patterns
209: 
210: ### 7.1 Pipeline Domain Integration
211: The Pipeline domain uses the Interaction backend for:
212: 1. **Stage Progress Reporting**: Emitting progress updates during long-running AI operations
213: 2. **Streaming Display**: Forwarding LLM token streams to the user interface
214: 3. **Confirmation Gates**: Pausing at stage boundaries for human approval
215: 
216: **Code Pattern:**
217: ```rust
218: // In stage_executor.rs
219: backend.send_streaming(content, &agent_name, is_thinking).await?;
220: if stage_config.requires_confirmation {
221:     let response = backend.request_input(prompt, options, None).await?;
222:     match response {
223:         InputResponse::Feedback(comment) => {
224:             return self.execute_with_feedback(comment).await;
225:         }
226:         // ... handle other variants
227:     }
228: }
229: ```
230: 
231: ### 7.2 Tools Domain Integration
232: Tools in the `hitl_content_tools.rs` and `hitl_file_tools.rs` modules use the backend to:
233: - Display files for review (`ReviewAndEditFileTool`)
234: - Collect structured feedback (`ReviewWithFeedbackTool`)
235: - Show tool execution confirmations
236: 
237: ### 7.3 Frontend Integration (GUI)
238: The React frontend (`crates/cowork-gui/src/App.tsx`) establishes event listeners before triggering execution:
239: 
240: ```javascript
241: // Event listeners setup
242: listen('agent_streaming', (event) => addStreamingMessage(event.payload));
243: listen('input_request', (event) => showInputModal(event.payload));
244: listen('tool_call', (event) => logToolInvocation(event.payload));
245: 
246: // Command invocation
247: invoke('gui_execute_iteration', { projectId, iterationId });
248: ```
249: 
250: ---
251: 
252: ## 8. Data Structures
253: 
254: ### 8.1 InputResponse Enum
255: Defines possible user responses to HITL requests:
256: - `Pass`: Continue without changes
257: - `Edit { content }`: Modified content from editor
258: - `Feedback { comment }`: Regeneration request with comments
259: - `Cancel`: Abort iteration
260: - `Timeout`: No response within 3000s window
261: 
262: ### 8.2 MessageType Enum
263: Categorizes messages for UI styling:
264: - `Normal`: Standard agent messages
265: - `Thinking`: Agent's internal reasoning (often rendered differently)
266: - `ToolCall`: Tool invocation notifications
267: - `ToolResult`: Success/failure indicators
268: - `Streaming`: Real-time LLM output fragments
269: 
270: ### 8.3 ProgressInfo Struct
271: ```rust
272: struct ProgressInfo {
273:     task_id: String,
274:     current: u32,
275:     total: u32,
276:     message: Option<String>,
277: }
278: ```
279: 
280: ---
281: 
282: ## 9. Configuration & Extension
283: 
284: ### 9.1 Implementing a New Backend
285: To add a new interface (e.g., WebSocket API):
286: 1. Implement `InteractiveBackend` for your struct
287: 2. Handle the `request_input` method with appropriate async synchronization
288: 3. Ensure UTF-8 compliance for all string inputs/outputs
289: 4. Implement timeout handling for HITL operations
290: 
291: ### 9.2 Backend Selection
292: The application selects the backend at runtime:
293: - **CLI**: `CliBackend` instantiated in `cowork-cli/src/main.rs`
294: - **GUI**: `TauriBackend` instantiated in `cowork-gui/src-tauri/src/lib.rs` with access to Tauri's `AppHandle`
295: 
296: ### 9.3 Security Considerations
297: - **Path Validation**: All file operations shown via `send_tool_call` have been pre-validated by Runtime Security
298: - **Input Sanitization**: The backend does not sanitize content (handled by Tools domain), but ensures proper encoding (UTF-8)
299: - **Timeout Protection**: Prevents resource exhaustion from abandoned HITL requests
300: 
301: ---
302: 
303: ## 10. Best Practices
304: 
305: 1. **Always Await Timeouts**: When implementing custom backends, ensure `request_input` implements timeout logic to prevent zombie pipelines
306: 2. **Preserve Context**: Pass `MessageContext` accurately to enable rich UI features in GUI mode while maintaining CLI compatibility
307: 3. **Non-Blocking Events**: Event emission should never block the AI pipeline; use `tokio::spawn` for fire-and-forget notifications if necessary
308: 4. **UTF-8 Compliance**: All implementations must handle Unicode correctly, particularly for international development teams
309: 5. **State Cleanup**: When implementing HITL, always remove completed request IDs from pending maps to prevent memory leaks
310: 
311: ---
312: 
313: ## 11. Associated Files
314: 
315: | File Path | Responsibility |
316: |-----------|---------------|
317: | `crates/cowork-core/src/interaction/mod.rs` | Trait definition and shared types |
318: | `crates/cowork-core/src/interaction/cli.rs` | Terminal-based implementation |
319: | `crates/cowork-core/src/interaction/tauri.rs` | Desktop GUI implementation |
320: | `crates/cowork-cli/src/main.rs` | CLI backend instantiation and routing |
321: | `crates/cowork-gui/src-tauri/src/lib.rs` | Tauri backend registration and state management |
322: | `crates/cowork-gui/src-tauri/src/gui_types.rs` | DTOs for frontend-backend communication |
323: 
324: ---
325: 
326: ## 12. Conclusion
327: 
328: The Interaction Domain exemplifies the system's adherence to Hexagonal Architecture by strictly separating interface concerns from business logic. Through the `InteractiveBackend` trait, Cowork Forge achieves true interface portability—enabling the same sophisticated AI pipeline to operate in both automated CI/CD environments (CLI) and rich interactive development environments (Desktop GUI) without code duplication or domain pollution.
329: 
330: The domain's sophisticated handling of asynchronous HITL workflows, combined with context-aware messaging and robust concurrency management, provides the foundation for reliable human-AI collaboration in software development workflows.
````

### litho.docs/en/4.Deep-Exploration/LLM Integration Domain.md (284 lines)

````
1: **LLM Integration Domain Technical Documentation**
2: 
3: ---
4: 
5: ## 1. Overview
6: 
7: The **LLM Integration Domain** provides the infrastructure layer for connecting Cowork Forge to OpenAI-compatible Large Language Model APIs. As an infrastructure domain within the Hexagonal Architecture, it abstracts the complexity of external API communication, authentication, and critical production concerns such as rate limiting and quota management.
8: 
9: This domain serves as the exclusive bridge between the core AI agent pipeline and external LLM providers, ensuring that the system maintains API compliance (30 requests/minute) while providing a clean, trait-based interface for the Pipeline Domain's Stage Executor and agent orchestration components.
10: 
11: **Key Responsibilities:**
12: - Configuration management supporting both file-based (TOML) and environment-based deployments
13: - Factory-based client instantiation with automatic rate limiting decoration
14: - Dual-mechanism rate limiting (concurrency control + temporal throttling) to prevent API quota exhaustion
15: - Seamless integration with the `adk-rust` ecosystem (`adk_core` traits and `adk_model` OpenAI client)
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 2. Architectural Design
20: 
21: ### 2.1 Pattern Implementation
22: 
23: The domain implements two primary design patterns to achieve clean separation of concerns and production-grade reliability:
24: 
25: **Factory Pattern**: The `create_llm_client` function acts as a centralized factory that orchestrates client initialization, configuration validation, and automatic wrapping with rate limiting decorators. This ensures that all LLM clients used throughout the system comply with API constraints by default.
26: 
27: **Decorator Pattern**: The `RateLimitedLlm` struct implements the `adk_core::Llm` trait while wrapping an inner LLM implementation. This allows rate limiting concerns to be added transparently without modifying the underlying OpenAI client logic, adhering to the Single Responsibility Principle.
28: 
29: ### 2.2 System Positioning
30: 
31: Within the Clean Architecture hierarchy, this domain resides in the **Infrastructure Layer**, depending only on external frameworks (`adk-rust`, `tokio`) and providing implementations for domain-defined interfaces:
32: 
33: ```mermaid
34: flowchart TD
35:     subgraph Infrastructure["Infrastructure Layer"]
36:         LLM["LLM Integration Domain"]
37:         Config["Configuration Management"]
38:         RateLimit["Rate Limiting<br/>Decorator"]
39:     end
40:     
41:     subgraph Application["Application Layer"]
42:         Pipeline["Pipeline Domain"]
43:         Executor["Stage Executor"]
44:     end
45:     
46:     subgraph External["External Systems"]
47:         OpenAI["OpenAI-compatible API"]
48:     end
49:     
50:     Pipeline -->|Uses| LLM
51:     LLM -->|Implements| Config
52:     LLM -->|Wraps| RateLimit
53:     RateLimit -->|Calls| OpenAI
54: ```
55: 
56: ---
57: 
58: ## 3. Core Components
59: 
60: ### 3.1 Configuration Management (`llm/config.rs`)
61: 
62: The configuration system supports dual-source initialization to accommodate both development workflows and containerized production environments:
63: 
64: **Configuration Structures:**
65: - `LlmConfig`: Top-level configuration container
66: - `ModelConfig`: Specific model parameters including API base URL, authentication key, and model selection
67: 
68: **Loading Strategies:**
69: - **File-based**: `ModelConfig::from_file(path)` deserializes TOML configuration files for persistent, version-controlled settings
70: - **Environment-based**: `ModelConfig::from_env()` reads from environment variables (`LLM_API_BASE_URL`, `LLM_API_KEY`, `LLM_MODEL_NAME`) for containerized deployments and secrets management
71: 
72: ### 3.2 Rate Limiting Infrastructure (`llm/rate_limiter.rs`)
73: 
74: The `RateLimitedLlm` decorator implements a sophisticated dual-mechanism rate limiting strategy essential for API quota compliance:
75: 
76: **Mechanism 1: Concurrency Control**
77: - Utilizes a global `tokio::sync::Semaphore` (initialized via `tokio::sync::OnceCell`) with a permit count of **1**
78: - Ensures only a single API request is in flight at any given time across the entire application
79: - Initialized lazily via `init_global_rate_limiter(max_concurrent: usize)`
80: 
81: **Mechanism 2: Temporal Throttling**
82: - Enforces a mandatory **2-second delay** (2000ms) before each API call using `tokio::time::sleep`
83: - Guarantees the system does not exceed **30 requests per minute** even under sustained load
84: - Implemented in `RateLimitedLlm::with_default_delay()` constructor
85: 
86: **Thread Safety:**
87: The global semaphore is stored in a `static OnceCell`, ensuring thread-safe, one-time initialization across async task boundaries without race conditions.
88: 
89: ### 3.3 Client Factory
90: 
91: The `create_llm_client` function serves as the primary entry point for obtaining LLM instances:
92: 
93: ```rust
94: pub fn create_llm_client(config: &LlmConfig) -> Result<Arc<dyn Llm>>
95: ```
96: 
97: **Orchestration Flow:**
98: 1. Initializes the global rate limiter semaphore (idempotent operation)
99: 2. Constructs `OpenAIConfig` with custom base URL support via `OpenAIConfig::compatible()`
100: 3. Instantiates `OpenAIClient` from the `adk_model` crate
101: 4. Wraps the client with `RateLimitedLlm` using the default 2000ms delay
102: 5. Returns an `Arc<dyn Llm>` suitable for dependency injection into agent components
103: 
104: ---
105: 
106: ## 4. Rate Limiting Strategy Deep Dive
107: 
108: The 30 requests/minute limit is enforced through a defense-in-depth approach combining concurrency and temporal controls:
109: 
110: ```mermaid
111: sequenceDiagram
112:     participant Agent as Pipeline Agent
113:     participant RL as RateLimitedLlm
114:     participant Sem as Global Semaphore
115:     participant API as LLM Provider
116:     
117:     Agent->>RL: generate_content(request)
118:     RL->>Sem: acquire().await
119:     Note over Sem: Permit obtained<br/>(max 1 concurrent)
120:     
121:     RL->>RL: sleep(2000ms)
122:     Note over RL: Temporal spacing<br/>30 req/min max
123:     
124:     RL->>API: HTTP Request
125:     API-->>RL: Response Stream
126:     RL-->>Agent: LlmResponseStream
127:     
128:     Note over Sem: Permit released<br/>on scope exit
129: ```
130: 
131: **Why Dual Mechanisms?**
132: - **Concurrency control** prevents burst traffic that could trigger provider rate limits or connection pool exhaustion
133: - **Temporal throttling** ensures steady-state compliance with per-minute quotas, even during sequential operations
134: - The combination provides graceful degradation under load while maintaining predictable API usage costs
135: 
136: ---
137: 
138: ## 5. Integration with Agent System
139: 
140: ### 5.1 Trait Implementation
141: 
142: The domain implements the `adk_core::Llm` trait, providing the standard interface for the agent framework:
143: 
144: ```rust
145: #[async_trait]
146: impl Llm for RateLimitedLlm {
147:     fn name(&self) -> &str;
148:     async fn generate_content(
149:         &self, 
150:         request: LlmRequest, 
151:         stream: bool
152:     ) -> Result<LlmResponseStream>;
153: }
154: ```
155: 
156: ### 5.2 Pipeline Domain Consumption
157: 
158: The Pipeline Domain's `StageExecutor` consumes LLM clients through dependency injection:
159: 
160: - **Initialization**: The executor receives an `Arc<dyn Llm>` via the factory function during pipeline context setup
161: - **Execution**: During stage execution (Idea, PRD, Design, etc.), the executor calls `generate_content()` with stage-specific instructions
162: - **Streaming**: Supports both streaming responses (for real-time GUI updates) and synchronous completion modes
163: - **Feedback Loops**: The Actor-Critic pattern relies on the rate-limited client for both initial generation and regeneration cycles
164: 
165: ### 5.3 Error Handling
166: 
167: All errors are propagated using the `anyhow` crate for ergonomic error handling:
168: - Configuration parsing errors (invalid TOML, missing env vars)
169: - HTTP connection failures
170: - Rate limiting semaphore poisoning (extremely unlikely with tokio's implementation)
171: - API authentication errors
172: 
173: ---
174: 
175: ## 6. Configuration Reference
176: 
177: ### 6.1 TOML Configuration File
178: 
179: ```toml
180: [llm]
181: api_base_url = "https://api.openai.com/v1"
182: api_key = "sk-..."
183: model_name = "gpt-4"
184: max_retries = 3
185: timeout_seconds = 120
186: ```
187: 
188: ### 6.2 Environment Variables
189: 
190: | Variable | Description | Required |
191: |----------|-------------|----------|
192: | `LLM_API_BASE_URL` | Base URL for OpenAI-compatible API | Yes |
193: | `LLM_API_KEY` | Authentication key | Yes |
194: | `LLM_MODEL_NAME` | Model identifier (e.g., gpt-4, gpt-3.5-turbo) | Yes |
195: 
196: **Precedence**: When both TOML and environment variables are present, the specific loading method called by the application determines which takes precedence. The factory pattern typically prioritizes explicit configuration objects over environment fallbacks.
197: 
198: ---
199: 
200: ## 7. Usage Patterns
201: 
202: ### 7.1 Initialization (Application Startup)
203: 
204: ```rust
205: use cowork_core::llm::{create_llm_client, LlmConfig};
206: 
207: // Load configuration
208: let config = LlmConfig::from_file("config.toml")?;
209: 
210: // Create rate-limited client
211: let llm_client = create_llm_client(&config)?;
212: // Client is now ready for injection into Pipeline Domain
213: ```
214: 
215: ### 7.2 Runtime Usage (Agent Execution)
216: 
217: ```rust
218: // Inside StageExecutor or Agent implementation
219: let response = llm_client
220:     .generate_content(request, true)  // true = streaming
221:     .await?;
222: 
223: // Process streaming chunks
224: while let Some(chunk) = response.stream.next().await {
225:     // Emit to GUI via Interaction Domain or process locally
226: }
227: ```
228: 
229: ### 7.3 Custom Rate Limiting (Advanced)
230: 
231: For scenarios requiring different rate limits (e.g., different providers with varying quotas):
232: 
233: ```rust
234: use cowork_core::llm::RateLimitedLlm;
235: 
236: // Custom delay: 1 second (60 req/min)
237: let custom_limited = RateLimitedLlm::new(inner_client, 1000);
238: ```
239: 
240: ---
241: 
242: ## 8. Operational Considerations
243: 
244: ### 8.1 API Quota Management
245: 
246: The default 30 req/min limit is conservative for standard OpenAI tiers. For enterprise tiers with higher limits:
247: - Modify the delay constant in `with_default_delay()` (currently 2000ms)
248: - Adjust the global semaphore count if the provider supports concurrent connections
249: - Monitor logs for rate limit responses (HTTP 429) as fallback protection
250: 
251: ### 8.2 Latency Implications
252: 
253: The 2-second mandatory delay introduces baseline latency:
254: - **Sequential operations**: A 7-stage pipeline with single-pass execution incurs minimum 14 seconds of rate-limiting overhead
255: - **Feedback loops**: Actor-Critic iterations multiply this overhead
256: - **Mitigation**: The delay is non-blocking for other application components due to Tokio's async runtime
257: 
258: ### 8.3 Monitoring and Observability
259: 
260: Key metrics to monitor:
261: - **Semaphore wait time**: Indicates queue depth if multiple agents request LLM access simultaneously
262: - **Request duration**: Total time including the 2-second delay and API latency
263: - **Error rates**: Authentication failures or rate limit breaches (HTTP 429)
264: 
265: ---
266: 
267: ## 9. File Structure
268: 
269: ```
270: crates/cowork-core/src/llm/
271: ├── mod.rs              # Module exports and public API
272: ├── config.rs           # LlmConfig, ModelConfig, factory function
273: └── rate_limiter.rs     # RateLimitedLlm decorator, global semaphore
274: ```
275: 
276: **Related Files:**
277: - `crates/cowork-core/src/tech_stack.rs`: Technology detection (auxiliary to LLM operations)
278: - `crates/cowork-core/src/pipeline/stage_executor.rs`: Primary consumer of LLM clients
279: 
280: ---
281: 
282: ## 10. Summary
283: 
284: The LLM Integration Domain provides a robust, production-ready bridge to external AI services while enforcing critical operational constraints. By encapsulating rate limiting logic within a decorator pattern and supporting flexible configuration sources, it enables the Pipeline Domain to focus on orchestration logic without concern for API compliance details. The dual-mechanism rate limiting (concurrency + temporal) ensures reliable operation within provider quotas, making it suitable for long-running autonomous agent pipelines as well as interactive GUI-driven development workflows.
````

### litho.docs/en/4.Deep-Exploration/Memory Domain.md (476 lines)

````
1: **Memory Domain Technical Documentation**
2: 
3: **Version:** 1.0  
4: **Last Updated:** 2025-01-09  
5: **Module:** `cowork-core/src/domain/memory`  
6: **Classification:** Core Business Domain
7: 
8: ---
9: 
10: ## 1. Overview
11: 
12: The **Memory Domain** provides the institutional knowledge management system for Cowork Forge, enabling project continuity across multiple development iterations through a sophisticated two-tier memory architecture. This domain implements the Repository pattern with Aggregate Root design to persist, query, and promote knowledge artifacts generated during the AI-driven development pipeline.
13: 
14: ### 1.1 Purpose and Value
15: 
16: - **Knowledge Persistence**: Maintains architectural decisions, design patterns, and iteration-specific insights across the software development lifecycle
17: - **Context Continuity**: Enables evolutionary development by providing AI agents with historical context from previous iterations
18: - **Knowledge Elevation**: Implements promotion mechanisms that transform transient iteration insights into permanent project-level decisions and reusable patterns
19: - **Retrieval Intelligence**: Supports fuzzy keyword search, tag-based filtering, and LLM-optimized context generation
20: 
21: ### 1.2 Architectural Position
22: 
23: The Memory Domain sits at the intersection of the Pipeline Domain (generating knowledge) and the Tools Domain (consuming knowledge). It serves as the system's "collective memory," accessible by both AI agents during execution and human users through the GUI/CLI interfaces.
24: 
25: ---
26: 
27: ## 2. Architecture & Design Principles
28: 
29: ### 2.1 Domain-Driven Design Structure
30: 
31: The Memory Domain follows **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)** principles with clear aggregate boundaries:
32: 
33: ```
34: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
35: │                    ProjectMemory                             │
36: │                  (Aggregate Root)                            │
37: ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
38: │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  │
39: │  │  Decisions   │  │   Patterns   │  │ ProjectContext   │  │
40: │  │   [Vec]      │  │    [Vec]     │  │                  │  │
41: │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  │
42: │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
43: │  │        IterationKnowledge [HashMap<iteration_id>]      │ │
44: │  │  ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────────┐    │ │
45: │  │  │   Insights   │    Issues    │    Learnings     │    │ │
46: │  │  └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────────┘    │ │
47: │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
48: └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
49: ```
50: 
51: ### 2.2 Two-Tier Memory Architecture
52: 
53: The system implements a bifurcated storage model:
54: 
55: **Tier 1: Project-Level Memory** (`ProjectMemory`)
56: - **Scope**: Global across all iterations
57: - **Lifespan**: Persistent for project duration
58: - **Contents**: Architectural Decisions, Design Patterns, Project Context
59: - **Storage**: `memory/project/memory.json`
60: 
61: **Tier 2: Iteration-Level Memory** (`IterationKnowledge` / `IterationMemory`)
62: - **Scope**: Specific to a single iteration
63: - **Lifespan**: Rotational (subject to retention policies)
64: - **Contents**: Insights, Issues, Learnings, Compressed stage summaries
65: - **Storage**: `memory/iterations/{iteration_id}.json`
66: 
67: ### 2.3 Repository Pattern Implementation
68: 
69: The `MemoryStore` acts as the repository interface, abstracting JSON file persistence details from domain logic. It provides:
70: - Aggregate persistence for `ProjectMemory`
71: - CRUD operations for iteration-specific memories
72: - Query engine with filtering and merging capabilities
73: - Knowledge promotion orchestration
74: 
75: ---
76: 
77: ## 3. Core Domain Models
78: 
79: ### 3.1 ProjectMemory (Aggregate Root)
80: 
81: The central aggregate encapsulating all project-level knowledge:
82: 
83: ```rust
84: pub struct ProjectMemory {
85:     pub project_id: String,
86:     pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
87:     pub updated_at: DateTime<Utc>,
88:     pub decisions: Vec<Decision>,
89:     pub patterns: Vec<Pattern>,
90:     pub context: ProjectContext,
91:     pub iteration_knowledge: HashMap<String, IterationKnowledge>,
92: }
93: ```
94: 
95: **Key Behaviors:**
96: - `query_decisions(keyword)`: Fuzzy search across decision titles and contexts
97: - `query_patterns(tag)`: Filter patterns by category tags
98: - `save_iteration_knowledge()`: Cache compressed iteration summaries
99: - `cleanup_old_knowledge(keep_count)`: Enforce retention policies
100: 
101: ### 3.2 IterationKnowledge
102: 
103: Compressed snapshot of an iteration's essential information for quick context loading:
104: 
105: ```rust
106: pub struct IterationKnowledge {
107:     pub iteration_id: String,
108:     pub summaries: DocumentSummaries,  // Compressed idea, PRD, design, plan
109:     pub tech_stack: TechStackMetadata,
110:     pub key_decisions: Vec<String>,    // References to project decisions
111:     pub key_patterns: Vec<String>,     // References to project patterns
112:     pub code_structure: CodeStructureSummary,
113:     pub known_issues: Vec<String>,
114:     pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
115: }
116: ```
117: 
118: ### 3.3 IterationMemory
119: 
120: Detailed capture of iteration execution telemetry:
121: 
122: ```rust
123: pub struct IterationMemory {
124:     pub iteration_id: String,
125:     pub insights: Vec<Insight>,    // AI-generated observations
126:     pub issues: Vec<Issue>,        // Problems encountered
127:     pub learnings: Vec<Learning>,  // Technical discoveries
128:     pub stage_results: Vec<StageResult>,
129: }
130: ```
131: 
132: ### 3.4 Value Objects
133: 
134: **Decision**: Captured architectural choices with consequences
135: ```rust
136: pub struct Decision {
137:     pub id: String,              // Format: dec-{iteration_id}-{uuid}
138:     pub title: String,
139:     pub context: String,
140:     pub decision: String,
141:     pub consequences: Vec<String>,
142:     pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
143:     pub iteration_id: Option<String>,  // Source iteration if promoted
144: }
145: ```
146: 
147: **ID Generation**: Decision IDs use UUID v4 format (`dec-{iteration_id}-{uuid}`) to ensure global uniqueness. This replaced the previous timestamp-based approach to prevent ID collisions in high-concurrency or rapid-creation scenarios.
148: 
149: **Pattern**: Reusable design solutions
150: ```rust
151: pub struct Pattern {
152:     pub id: String,              // Format: pat-{iteration_id}-{uuid}
153:     pub name: String,
154:     pub description: String,
155:     pub usage_examples: Vec<String>,
156:     pub tags: Vec<String>,
157:     pub code_example: Option<String>,
158:     pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
159: }
160: ```
161: 
162: **ID Generation**: Pattern IDs follow the same UUID v4 format (`pat-{iteration_id}-{uuid}`) for consistent uniqueness guarantees.
163: 
164: **Insight**: Transient observations with importance levels
165: ```rust
166: pub struct Insight {
167:     pub id: String,
168:     pub content: String,
169:     pub stage: PipelineStage,
170:     pub importance: ImportanceLevel,  // Critical, Important, Normal
171:     pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
172:     pub promoted_to_decision: bool,
173: }
174: ```
175: 
176: ---
177: 
178: ## 4. Persistence Layer
179: 
180: ### 4.1 Storage Structure
181: 
182: ```
183: .cowork-v2/
184: └── memory/
185:     ├── project/
186:     │   └── memory.json          # ProjectMemory aggregate
187:     └── iterations/
188:         ├── iter_001.json        # IterationMemory for iteration 1
189:         ├── iter_002.json        # IterationMemory for iteration 2
190:         └── ...
191: ```
192: 
193: ### 4.2 MemoryStore Interface
194: 
195: The `MemoryStore` provides the primary API for persistence operations:
196: 
197: **Project Memory Operations:**
198: - `load_project_memory(project_id) -> Result<ProjectMemory>`
199: - `save_project_memory(&ProjectMemory) -> Result<()>`
200: - `add_decision(project_id, Decision) -> Result<()>`
201: - `add_pattern(project_id, Pattern) -> Result<()>`
202: 
203: **Iteration Memory Operations:**
204: - `load_iteration_memory(iteration_id) -> Result<IterationMemory>`
205: - `save_iteration_memory(&IterationMemory) -> Result<()>`
206: 
207: **Query Operations:**
208: - `query(&MemoryQuery, iteration_id: Option<&str>) -> Result<MemoryQueryResult>`
209: 
210: ### 4.3 Serialization Strategy
211: 
212: - **Format**: Human-readable pretty-printed JSON (`serde_json::to_string_pretty`)
213: - **Temporal Tracking**: ISO 8601 UTC timestamps via `chrono::DateTime<Utc>`
214: - **Schema Evolution**: Forward-compatible struct definitions with `#[serde(default)]`
215: 
216: ---
217: 
218: ## 5. Query Engine
219: 
220: ### 5.1 Query Scopes
221: 
222: The query engine supports three semantic scopes:
223: 
224: | Scope | Description | Use Case |
225: |-------|-------------|----------|
226: | `Project` | Query only project-level decisions and patterns | Historical context retrieval |
227: | `Iteration` | Query only specific iteration insights/issues | Debugging specific iteration |
228: | `Smart` | Merged view: Project memory + latest iteration knowledge | Active development context |
229: 
230: ### 5.2 Query Types
231: 
232: ```rust
233: pub enum MemoryQueryType {
234:     Decisions,    // Architectural decisions only
235:     Patterns,     // Design patterns only
236:     Insights,     // AI observations only
237:     Issues,       // Known problems only
238:     Learnings,    // Technical discoveries only
239:     All,          // Unified search across all types
240: }
241: ```
242: 
243: ### 5.3 Search Capabilities
244: 
245: **Fuzzy Keyword Search:**
246: - Case-insensitive substring matching on titles and content
247: - Tokenization of search terms for partial matches
248: - Relevance scoring for result ranking
249: 
250: **Tag-Based Filtering:**
251: - Pattern retrieval by category tags (e.g., "architecture", "performance", "security")
252: - Multi-tag intersection support
253: 
254: **Temporal Filtering:**
255: - Date range constraints for knowledge retrieval
256: - "Latest N" iteration limiting
257: 
258: ### 5.4 LLM Context Generation
259: 
260: The `MemoryQueryResult` provides `to_context_string()` method that formats search results into optimized prompts for LLM consumption:
261: 
262: ```rust
263: impl MemoryQueryResult {
264:     pub fn to_context_string(&self) -> String {
265:         // Formats decisions, patterns, and insights
266:         // into structured markdown suitable for 
267:         // inclusion in AI agent prompts
268:     }
269: }
270: ```
271: 
272: ---
273: 
274: ## 6. Knowledge Promotion
275: 
276: ### 6.1 Promotion Workflow
277: 
278: Knowledge promotion elevates transient iteration insights to permanent project assets:
279: 
280: ```
281: ┌──────────────┐     Critical Importance      ┌──────────────┐
282: │   Insight    │ ───────────────────────────> │   Decision   │
283: │  (Iteration) │    [PromoteToDecisionTool]   │   (Project)  │
284: └──────────────┘                              └──────────────┘
285: 
286: ┌──────────────┐     Reusable Solution         ┌──────────────┐
287: │   Learning   │ ───────────────────────────> │    Pattern   │
288: │  (Iteration) │    [PromoteToPatternTool]    │   (Project)  │
289: └──────────────┘                              └──────────────┘
290: ```
291: 
292: ### 6.2 Automatic Promotion
293: 
294: The system implements automatic promotion for **Critical** importance insights:
295: - During iteration finalization, `promote_insights_to_decisions()` scans for insights marked `ImportanceLevel::Critical`
296: - Automatically creates Decision entries with back-references to source iteration
297: - Prevents knowledge loss from high-impact observations
298: 
299: ### 6.3 Manual Promotion Tools
300: 
301: **PromoteToDecisionTool**: Elevates insights to architectural decisions
302: - Parameters: title, context, decision text, consequences array
303: - Validation: Ensures uniqueness of decision titles
304: - Side Effects: Updates ProjectMemory and persists to disk
305: 
306: **PromoteToPatternTool**: Creates reusable design patterns
307: - Parameters: name, description, usage examples, tags, optional code example
308: - Validation: Checks for naming conflicts
309: - Indexing: Adds to searchable pattern registry
310: 
311: ---
312: 
313: ## 7. Tool Integration (ADK)
314: 
315: The Memory Domain exposes 10 specialized tools through the Agent Development Kit (ADK) for AI agent interaction:
316: 
317: ### 7.1 Memory Tools (`memory_tools.rs`)
318: 
319: | Tool | Purpose | Target Tier |
320: |------|---------|-------------|
321: | `QueryMemoryTool` | Flexible memory retrieval with scope/type filtering | Both |
322: | `SaveInsightTool` | Capture AI observations during execution | Iteration |
323: | `SaveIssueTool` | Record problems encountered | Iteration |
324: | `SaveLearningTool` | Document technical discoveries | Iteration |
325: | `PromoteToDecisionTool` | Elevate insight to project decision | Project |
326: | `PromoteToPatternTool` | Create reusable pattern | Project |
327: 
328: **QueryMemoryTool Parameters:**
329: ```rust
330: pub struct QueryMemoryParams {
331:     pub scope: MemoryScope,        // Project, Iteration, Smart
332:     pub query_type: MemoryQueryType, // Decisions, Patterns, Insights, All
333:     pub keywords: Vec<String>,     // Fuzzy search terms
334:     pub limit: Option<usize>,      // Result limiting
335:     pub iteration_id: Option<String>, // Required for Iteration scope
336: }
337: ```
338: 
339: ### 7.2 Knowledge Tools (`knowledge_tools.rs`)
340: 
341: | Tool | Purpose |
342: |------|---------|
343: | `LoadDocumentSummaryTool` | Retrieve compressed stage summaries (idea, PRD, design, plan) |
344: | `LoadBaseKnowledgeTool` | Load historical knowledge from base iterations |
345: | `SaveKnowledgeSnapshotTool` | Persist complete knowledge state with metadata |
346: | `ListFilesWorkspaceTool` | Workspace introspection with metadata extraction |
347: 
348: ---
349: 
350: ## 8. Integration Patterns
351: 
352: ### 8.1 Pipeline Integration
353: 
354: During the 7-stage pipeline execution:
355: 1. **Capture**: Agents use `SaveInsightTool` during stages to record observations
356: 2. **Aggregation**: At iteration completion, `SaveKnowledgeSnapshotTool` compresses stage artifacts into `IterationKnowledge`
357: 3. **Inheritance**: Evolution iterations use `LoadBaseKnowledgeTool` to retrieve context from previous iterations
358: 4. **Context Injection**: `QueryMemoryTool` provides historical decisions/patterns to agent prompts
359: 
360: ### 8.2 GUI Integration
361: 
362: The Memory Domain supports the React-based MemoryPanel:
363: - **Real-time Queries**: `query_memory_index` Tauri command delegates to `MemoryStore`
364: - **Visualization**: Category-based color coding (Decisions=blue, Patterns=green, Insights=yellow)
365: - **Detail Views**: Markdown rendering of full content with temporal metadata
366: - **Promotion UI**: Workflow for converting insights to decisions via modal interfaces
367: 
368: ### 8.3 Retention Management
369: 
370: The `cleanup_old_knowledge(keep_count)` method implements rotational storage:
371: - Maintains only the most recent N iterations in detail (`IterationMemory`)
372: - Preserves compressed summaries (`IterationKnowledge`) indefinitely
373: - Project-level decisions and patterns persist forever
374: 
375: ---
376: 
377: ## 9. Data Flow Examples
378: 
379: ### 9.1 Knowledge Capture Flow
380: 
381: ```mermaid
382: sequenceDiagram
383:     participant Agent as AI Agent (Coding Stage)
384:     participant SaveTool as SaveInsightTool
385:     participant Store as MemoryStore
386:     participant File as File System
387:     
388:     Agent->>SaveTool: save_insight(stage=Coding, content="...", importance=Critical)
389:     SaveTool->>Store: load_iteration_memory(iter_id)
390:     Store->>File: Read iterations/iter_003.json
391:     File-->>Store: JSON content
392:     Store->>Store: Deserialize -> IterationMemory
393:     Store->>Store: Push Insight to insights vector
394:     Store->>File: Write updated JSON
395:     File-->>Store: Success
396:     Store-->>SaveTool: Ok(())
397:     SaveTool-->>Agent: Success JSON
398: ```
399: 
400: ### 9.2 Cross-Iteration Query Flow
401: 
402: ```mermaid
403: sequenceDiagram
404:     participant User as GUI User
405:     participant Panel as MemoryPanel
406:     participant Cmd as Tauri Command
407:     participant Store as MemoryStore
408:     participant PM as ProjectMemory
409:     
410:     User->>Panel: Select scope smart query type decisions
411:     Panel->>Cmd: Invoke query memory index command
412:     Cmd->>Store: Query memory with parameters
413:     
414:     Store->>File: Read project memory JSON file
415:     File-->>Store: Project memory data
416:     Store->>PM: Deserialize data
417:     Store->>PM: Query decisions with keyword auth
418:     PM-->>Store: Return filtered decisions
419:     
420:     Store->>Store: Convert to context string
421:     Store-->>Cmd: Return memory query result
422:     Cmd-->>Panel: Return JSON response
423:     Panel->>Panel: Render decision cards with markdown
424: ```
425: 
426: ---
427: 
428: ## 10. Configuration & Constraints
429: 
430: ### 10.1 Storage Constraints
431: 
432: - **Format**: JSON files with pretty printing (human-readable)
433: - **Size Limits**: No hard limits, but queries implement `limit` parameters for performance
434: - **Concurrency**: File-based locking prevents corruption during concurrent access
435: - **Path Validation**: All paths constrained to project workspace (`.cowork-v2/memory/`)
436: 
437: ### 10.2 Retention Policies
438: 
439: - **Default Iteration Keep Count**: 10 (configurable via `cleanup_old_knowledge`)
440: - **Compression**: `IterationKnowledge` maintains 1000-character summaries of stage documents
441: - **Archival**: No automatic deletion of project-level memories (decisions/patterns)
442: 
443: ### 10.3 Performance Characteristics
444: 
445: - **Query Latency**: < 100ms for typical projects (< 50 iterations)
446: - **Memory Footprint**: ProjectMemory loaded once per session, iteration memories loaded on demand
447: - **Indexing**: In-memory HashMap for iteration knowledge lookups; linear scan for text search
448: 
449: ---
450: 
451: ## 11. Best Practices
452: 
453: ### 11.1 For Agent Developers
454: 
455: 1. **Insight Granularity**: Save insights at task completion boundaries, not per operation
456: 2. **Importance Levels**: Reserve `Critical` for architectural implications triggering promotion
457: 3. **Pattern Completeness**: When promoting learnings to patterns, include concrete usage examples
458: 
459: ### 11.2 For System Integrators
460: 
461: 1. **Query Scope Selection**: Use `Smart` scope for active development; `Project` for architectural planning
462: 2. **Context Window Management**: Respect `limit` parameters to prevent LLM context overflow
463: 3. **Promotion Timing**: Promote insights to decisions during iteration review, not during active coding
464: 
465: ---
466: 
467: ## 12. Related Documentation
468: 
469: - **Pipeline Domain**: Stage execution and agent orchestration
470: - **Tools Domain**: ADK tool implementation patterns
471: - **Persistence Domain**: JSON store implementation details
472: - **GUI Frontend**: MemoryPanel component architecture
473: 
474: ---
475: 
476: *This documentation reflects the Memory Domain implementation as of the current system version. For implementation details, refer to the source files listed in the associated files section.*
````

### litho.docs/zh/1、项目概述.md (290 lines)

````
1: # 项目概述：Cowork Forge 系统上下文
2: 
3: ## 1. 执行摘要
4: 
5: **Cowork Forge** 是一个 AI 原生的迭代式软件开发平台，通过结构化的七阶段开发流程编排自主多智能体工作流。该系统利用大型语言模型（LLM）将自然语言想法转化为生产就绪的软件，同时通过复杂的内存管理系统维护项目连续性。
6: 
7: 作为基于 Rust 架构构建的独立桌面应用程序，Cowork Forge 提供两种交互模式：用于自动化工作流的命令行界面和用于交互式开发的基于 Tauri 的图形界面。该平台采用领域驱动设计（DDD）原则和六边形架构模式，确保核心业务逻辑、基础设施关注点和表示层之间的清晰分离。
8: 
9: ## 2. 系统概述
10: 
11: ### 2.1 核心目标
12: 
13: Cowork Forge 解决了在 AI 辅助软件开发中维护架构一致性和上下文连续性的挑战。系统的主要目标包括：
14: 
15: - **自主开发编排**：通过确定的 7 阶段流程（创意 → PRD → 设计 → 计划 → 编码 → 检查 → 交付）自动化从概念化到交付的完整软件开发生命周期
16: - **上下文内存保留**：通过持久性内存聚合在开发迭代中维护组织知识，支持三种继承模式的演进开发（完整、部分、无）
17: - **人在回路治理**：在关键阶段实施验证门，确保人工监督以进行质量控制和架构对齐
18: - **双接口可访问性**：通过共享核心域同时支持自动化 CLI 工作流和交互式 GUI 体验
19: 
20: ### 2.2 业务价值
21: 
22: 该平台通过以下方式提供可衡量的价值：
23: 
24: | 价值主张 | 描述 |
25: |-------------------|-------------|
26: | **开发加速** | 通过自动化需求文档、架构设计和初始代码脚手架生成，减少样板代码生成时间 |
27: | **架构一致性** | 通过 Critic 智能体根据质量标准验证输出的 Actor-Critic 模式强制执行标准化工作流 |
28: | **知识保留** | 在迭代中保留架构决策、设计模式和技术见解，防止长时间运行项目中的上下文丢失 |
29: | **标准化方法论** | 通过结构化阶段门和验证协议为团队提供一致的开发实践 |
30: | **本地执行** | 完全在本地基础设施上运行，无需云依赖，确保数据隐私并降低运营成本 |
31: | **外部能力扩展** | 通过 MCP 协议集成 Tavily 搜索、DeepWiki 文档等外部智能体能力，扩展工具生态，增强信息检索和知识查询能力 |
32: 
33: ### 2.3 技术特性
34: 
35: **架构模式**：六边形/端口和适配器，采用领域驱动设计  
36: **核心技术栈**： 
37: - **后端**：Rust（Tokio 异步运行时），带 adk-rust 智能体框架
38: - **前端**：React 18 配合 Ant Design（GUI）、clap（CLI）
39: - **桌面外壳**：用于跨平台原生能力的 Tauri 运行时
40: - **持久化**：基于 JSON 的文件存储，包含工作区隔离
41: 
42: **关键技术特性**：
43: - **外部集成**：Model Context Protocol (MCP) HTTP 客户端 (adk-tool)
44: - **限流 LLM 集成**：装饰器模式实现，每分钟 30 次请求且单一并发控制
45: - **事件驱动通信**：通过事件发射在 Tauri 后端和 React 前端之间进行实时双向 IPC
46: - **安全优先操作**：所有文件系统操作的路径验证和工作区隔离
47: - **内存中心设计**：通过持久性内存聚合（决策、模式、知识快照）实现项目连续性
48: 
49: ## 3. 目标用户和利益相关者
50: 
51: ### 3.1 个人开发者
52: 
53: **画像**：在原型开发和样板减少方面寻求生产力提升的软件开发人员和技术负责人。
54: 
55: **使用场景**：
56: - 根据自然语言描述进行快速原型设计
57: - 自动生成项目需求和技术文档
58: - 通过演进迭代对现有代码库进行迭代优化
59: - 具有集成预览功能的本地开发环境管理
60: 
61: **关键需求**：
62: - 双接口灵活性（CLI 用于脚本化，GUI 用于探索）
63: - 最小配置开销和自动技术检测
64: - 在自动化执行期间暂停和干预的能力
65: - 本地工件存储和对生成输出的完全透明
66: 
67: ### 3.2 开发团队
68: 
69: **画像**：需要跨多个项目标准化工作流和知识保留的中小型开发团队。
70: 
71: **使用场景**：
72: - 使用标准化项目初始化来帮助新团队成员入职
73: - 跨多个微服务或模块维护架构一致性
74: - 为组织知识库保留设计决策和模式
75: - 通过共享项目内存协作审查 AI 生成的工件
76: 
77: **关键需求**：
78: - 具有技术栈检测的多项目管理
79: - 标准化开发方法论执行
80: - 团队成员和迭代之间的知识保留
81: - 关键架构决策的人工在回路验证
82: 
83: ### 3.3 AI 增强开发者
84: 
85: **画像**：探索 AI 辅助开发方法论并需要了解 AI 决策过程的技术早期采用者。
86: 
87: **使用场景**：
88: - 使用完整审计跟踪试验 AI 生成的架构设计
89: - 为智能体提供反馈以迭代优化输出
90: - 通过逐阶段执行监控研究 AI 推理模式
91: - 根据安全和性能标准验证 AI 生成的代码
92: 
93: **关键需求**：
94: - 具有实时进度监控的透明智能体工作流
95: - 全面工件可见性（需求、设计、计划、代码）
96: - Actor-Critic 模式交互的反馈循环
97: - 每个流程阶段的继续或重新生成的控制机制
98: 
99: ## 4. 系统范围和边界
100: 
101: ### 4.1 系统范围定义
102: 
103: Cowork Forge 作为一个**独立的 AI 辅助开发环境**运行，生成项目工件、编写代码、验证实现并管理项目演进。系统完全在本地机器上运行，通过外部 LLM API 编排 AI 智能体，同时将所有项目状态、迭代历史和生成的工件保存在本地存储中。
104: 
105: ### 4.2 包含的组件
106: 
107: 系统边界涵盖以下核心架构组件：
108: 
109: | 组件类别 | 具体组件 |
110: |-------------------|---------------------|
111: | **核心域逻辑** | 项目和迭代聚合、生命周期管理、继承模式逻辑 |
112: | **AI 流程引擎** | 7 阶段工作流控制器、带 ADK 集成的阶段执行器、Actor-Critic 模式实现、模块化迭代执行器 |
113: | **智能体指令系统** | 约 2000 行提示工程（每个阶段的 Actor/Critic 指令、知识生成、Legacy Project Analyzer） |
114: | **配置系统** | 配置注册表、Agent/Stage/Flow/Skill/Integration 定义、用户配置管理、配置验证 |
115: | **技能模块** | adk-skill 集成（agentskills.io 标准）、技能发现、选择和注入 |
116: | **工具生态系统** | 40+ ADK 工具（文件工具、数据工具、验证工具、HITL 工具、内存工具、部署工具、Legacy 项目分析工具），支持 MCP 远程工具集成（Tavily、DeepWiki 等） |
117: | **内存管理** | 项目内存和迭代知识域、查询索引、知识提升工作流 |
118: | **专用智能体** | PM Agent 用于交付后交互、Legacy Project Analyzer 用于现有项目导入和逆向分析、知识生成智能体用于洞察提取 |
119: | **导入器模块** | 项目分析器、工件生成器、导入配置、技术栈检测 |
120: | **持久化层** | 项目存储、迭代存储、内存存储，采用基于 JSON 的存储和工作区目录结构（`.cowork-v2`） |
121: | **用户界面** | CLI 接口（基于 clap）、基于 Tauri 的 GUI（React 前端，9 面板界面） |
122: | **基础设施服务** | 限流 LLM 客户端工厂、InteractiveBackend 特性实现（CLI 和 Tauri）、用于开发服务器的 ProcessRunner |
123: 
124: ### 4.3 排除的组件
125: 
126: 以下组件在系统边界之外运行，视为外部依赖：
127: 
128: - **版本控制系统**：Git 操作和仓库管理
129: - **云基础设施**：托管服务、云部署平台、远程计算资源
130: - **CI/CD 流程**：持续集成和交付自动化
131: - **包注册表**：npm、crates.io、PyPI 和其他外部依赖仓库
132: - **LLM 训练基础设施**：模型训练、微调或托管基础设施
133: 
134: ## 5. 外部系统交互
135: 
136: ### 5.1 外部系统概览
137: 
138: ```mermaid
139: C4Context
140:     title 系统上下文图 - Cowork Forge
141: 
142:     Person(individual, "个人开发者", "使用 CLI 或 GUI 进行 AI 辅助开发")
143:     Person(team, "开发团队", "通过标准化 AI 工作流进行协作")
144:     
145:     System_Boundary(cf, "Cowork Forge") {
146:         System(core, "核心引擎", "域逻辑、流程执行、内存管理")
147:         System(gui, "桌面 GUI", "Tauri + React 交互式界面")
148:         System(cli, "CLI 接口", "命令行自动化工具")
149:     }
150:     
151:     System_Ext(llm, "LLM 提供商 API", "用于智能体编排的 OpenAI 兼容 API")
152:     System_Ext(fs, "本地文件系统", "工作区存储和工件持久化")
153:     System_Ext(shell, "Shell/命令执行器", "构建工具、开发服务器、系统命令")
154:     System_Ext(editor, "外部编辑器", "用于 HITL 内容审查的系统默认编辑器")
155:     System_Ext(devserver, "开发服务器", "Vite 等用于实时应用预览")
156:     System_Ext(tauri, "Tauri 运行时", "桌面 GUI 框架和原生 API")
157:     System_Ext(mcp_servers, "MCP 服务器", "提供外部 AI 能力（Tavily 搜索、DeepWiki 文档查询）")
158:     
159:     Rel(individual, gui, "通过桌面应用交互")
160:     Rel(individual, cli, "通过终端执行命令")
161:     Rel(team, gui, "通过共享项目内存协作")
162:     
163:     Rel(core, llm, "发送 API 请求\n(限流: 30 次/分钟)", "HTTPS/JSON")
164:     Rel(core, fs, "读写项目文件\n(工作区隔离)", "文件 I/O")
165:     Rel(core, mcp_servers, "查询外部工具和服务", "MCP/HTTP")
166:     Rel(core, shell, "执行验证命令\n和构建流程", "进程执行")
167:     Rel(core, editor, "调用进行内容\n审查和编辑", "系统调用")
168:     
169:     Rel(gui, tauri, "渲染 UI 并\n管理窗口状态", "运行时 API")
170:     Rel(gui, core, "通过\nTauri 命令编排", "内部 IPC")
171:     Rel(cli, core, "调用域\n操作", "库调用")
172:     
173:     Rel(gui, devserver, "管理生命周期\n和日志流式传输", "进程管理")
174:     Rel(devserver, fs, "服务生成的\n应用文件", "文件访问")
175:     
176:     UpdateLayoutConfig($c4ShapeInRow="3", $c4BoundaryInRow="1")
177: ```
178: 
179: ### 5.2 交互详情
180: 
181: #### 5.2.1 LLM 提供商 API
182: - **交互类型**：出站 API 调用（HTTPS/JSON）
183: - **目的**：基于智能体的代码生成、文档创建、架构决策和 Critic 验证
184: - **约束**：限流至每分钟 30 次请求，带并发控制（单一信号量）以确保 API 配额合规和成本控制
185: - **数据交换**：包含项目上下文和指令的提示；包含生成工件的流式响应
186: 
187: #### 5.2.2 本地文件系统
188: - **交互类型**：本地文件 I/O
189: - **目的**：工作区管理、工件存储、项目文件操作和内存持久化
190: - **安全模型**：路径验证和工作区隔离，确保所有操作保持在指定项目目录内（`.cowork-v2` 约定）
191: - **数据交换**：JSON 元数据文件、markdown 文档、源代码文件 和知识快照
192: 
193: #### 5.2.3 Shell/命令执行器
194: - **交互类型**：进程执行
195: - **目的**：项目验证（编译、测试）、依赖安装、构建流程和开发服务器管理
196: - **执行上下文**：跨平台命令执行，在项目工作区内具有工作目录隔离
197: - **数据交换**：命令 stdin/stdout/stderr 流、用于成功/失败判定的退出码
198: 
199: #### 5.2.4 外部编辑器
200: - **交互类型**：外部进程调用
201: - **目的**：人在回路内容审查工作流，用户在首选编辑器中编辑生成的内容
202: - **集成**：系统默认编辑器检测和调用，用于包含阶段输出的临时文件
203: - **数据交换**：临时内容文件的文件路径；用户修改保存回系统
204: 
205: #### 5.2.5 开发服务器
206: - **交互类型**：进程管理和监控
207: - **目的**：生成的前端应用程序的实时预览（如 Vite、Webpack 开发服务器）
208: - **生命周期管理**：进程生成、通过 Tauri 事件的日志流式传输、优雅关闭协调
209: - **数据交换**：实时 stdout/stderr 日志流式传输、应用资产的 HTTP 服务
210: 
211: #### 5.2.6 Tauri 运行时
212: - **交互类型**：运行时框架依赖
213: - **目的**：桌面 GUI 能力、窗口管理、跨平台原生 API，以及 Rust 后端和 JavaScript 前端之间的安全 IPC
214: - **架构角色**：为 React 前端提供表示层容器，支持系统托盘集成、原生菜单和安全上下文隔离
215: - **数据交换**：命令调用（invoke）、事件发射（智能体事件、流式响应）和二进制资产管理
216: 
217: #### 5.2.7 MCP 服务器
218: - **交互类型**：基于 HTTP 的协议集成
219: - **目的**：通过 Model Context Protocol (MCP) 扩展 Agents 的对外部 AI 服务的能力。当前支持 Tavily 网络搜索和 AI 驱动的研究，以及 DeepWiki 代码文档查询。
220: - **配置**：通过 `config.toml` 的 `[mcp]` 节管理，包含 `tavily_api_key` 和 `deepwiki_enabled` 标志。应用启动时自动初始化，连接配置的 MCP 服务器并将工具集注入所有 Agents。
221: - **数据交换**：MCP HTTP 请求/响应；注入后远程工具在 Agents 看来表现为原生 ADK 工具。
222: 
223: ## 6. 关键架构决策
224: 
225: ### 6.1 多阶段流程与 Actor-Critic 模式
226: 系统实现了一个确定的 7 阶段工作流（创意 → PRD → 设计 → 计划 → 编码 → 检查 → 交付），其中每个阶段采用 Actor-Critic 模式。这确保 AI 生成的输出在继续之前经过验证，反馈循环使质量标准不满足时能够重新生成。
227: 
228: ### 6.2 具有共享核心的双接口策略
229: 架构通过 `InteractiveBackend` 特性抽象同时支持 CLI 和 GUI 接口，确保业务逻辑与接口无关。这使得自动化优先的用户能够利用脚本化能力，而交互式用户则受益于实时可视化和控制。
230: 
231: ### 6.3 内存中心演进开发
232: 系统不是将每个开发会话视为孤立的，而是维护持久的内存聚合（项目内存、迭代知识），捕获决策、模式和见解。这支持三种继承模式（完整、部分、无）的演进迭代，支持复杂的重构和增量开发工作流。
233: 
234: ### 6.4 工作区隔离和安全
235: 所有文件操作都通过路径验证机制限制在指定的工作区目录内。这种安全优先的方法防止 AI 智能体访问或修改预期项目范围之外的文件，解决了自主代码生成中的安全问题。
236: 
237: ### 6.5 事件驱动 GUI 架构
238: 基于 Tauri 的 GUI 采用事件驱动模型进行实时执行监控。后端事件（智能体消息、工具调用、流式响应、进度更新）被发射到 React 前端，实现 AI 智能体活动的实时可视化，无需轮询开销。
239: 
240: ### 6.6 数据驱动配置系统
241: 引入了配置驱动的方法，Agent、Stage、Flow、Skill 和 Integration 定义为 JSON 配置而非硬编码实现。这使用户能够自定义开发工作流、创建专用智能体并扩展系统能力，而无需修改源代码。配置注册表管理内置和用户定义的配置，支持验证和热重载。
242: 
243: ### 6.7 现有项目导入与逆向分析
244: 
245: 系统支持导入任意现有项目（即使不是用 Cowork Forge 创建的项目），通过 Legacy Project Analyzer Agent 进行逆向分析。该智能体会扫描项目结构、检测技术栈、读取现有文档和关键源文件，然后使用 LLM 生成完整的文档（idea.md、prd.md、design.md、plan.md）。
246: 
247: **导入流程：**
248: 1. **项目分析**：扫描项目结构，检测技术栈，读取 README 和关键配置文件
249: 2. **工件生成**：通过 LLM Agent 或模板模式生成文档工件
250: 3. **项目初始化**：创建 Cowork Forge 项目结构和初始迭代记录
251: 
252: **技术实现：**
253: - `importer` 模块：项目分析器、工件生成器、导入配置
254: - Legacy Project Analyzer Agent：专用智能体，配备项目扫描工具
255: - CLI `import` 命令：支持 LLM 生成或模板生成两种模式
256: 
257: **CLI 使用示例：**
258: ```bash
259: # 导入项目并生成所有文档
260: cowork import /path/to/project --idea --prd --design --plan
261: 
262: # 仅使用模板生成（无需 LLM 配置）
263: cowork import /path/to/project --idea --template-only
264: ```
265: 
266: 这使得用户可以将现有代码库快速纳入 Cowork Forge 的迭代开发工作流程。
267: 
268: ### 6.8 交付后 PM Agent 集成
269: 迭代完成后，项目经理 Agent 提供交互式界面用于持续项目参与。PM Agent 实现意图识别来分类用户请求（bug 修复、需求变更、新功能、咨询）并执行相应操作（阶段导航、新迭代创建、问答响应）。这弥合了自动化流程执行和持续项目维护之间的差距。
270: 
271: ### 6.9 基于 agentskills.io 标准的技能扩展
272: 技能模块实现 agentskills.io 标准，用于领域特定能力注入。技能从 `.skills/` 目录发现，与用户查询语义匹配，并作为附加指令和工具注入到智能体上下文中。这支持在不修改核心系统的情况下进行可扩展的、社区驱动的专用能力开发。
273: 
274: ## 7. 技术栈总结
275: 
276: | 层级 | 技术 | 目的 |
277: |-------|-----------|---------|
278: | **核心域** | Rust、Tokio、adk-rust | 异步智能体编排、域逻辑 |
279: | **持久化** | JSON、文件系统 | 实体存储、内存索引 |
280: | **LLM 集成** | OpenAI 兼容 API、自定义限流器 | 带配额管理的智能体能力 |
281: | **CLI** | clap、dialoguer、colored | 带交互提示的命令行界面 |
282: | **GUI 后端** | Tauri、Rust | 桌面运行时、系统集成 |
283: | **GUI 前端** | React 18、Ant Design、Monaco Editor | 交互式用户界面、代码编辑 |
284: | **进程管理** | Tokio 进程、跨平台 shell | 开发服务器控制、构建执行 |
285: 
286: ---
287: 
288: **文档版本**：1.0  
289: **分类**：架构文档（C4 系统上下文级别）  
290: **下一步**：请参阅容器和组件级图表以获取详细的内部架构文档。
````

### litho.docs/zh/2、架构概览.md (1242 lines)

````
1: # 系统架构文档：Cowork Forge
2: 
3: **文档版本**：1.0  
4: **生成时间**：2026-02-14 05:10:16 (UTC)  
5: **分类**：架构概述  
6: **目标受众**：软件架构师、高级开发人员、DevOps 工程师
7: 
8: ---
9: 
10: ## 1. 架构概述
11: 
12: ### 1.1 设计理念
13: 
14: Cowork Forge 体现了一种**混合 AI-人类协作架构**，旨在编排自主软件开发，同时通过结构化干预点保持人类监督。架构基于三个基本原则构建：
15: 
16: 1. **认知增强**：系统作为人类开发者认知的延伸，通过 AI 智能体在迭代中保留组织知识，同时自动化机械开发任务。
17: 
18: 2. **结构化自主**：而非无约束的 AI 生成，系统强制执行严格的 7 阶段流程（创意→PRD→设计→计划→编码→检查→交付），带有验证门，通过 Actor-Critic 模式确保质量保证。
19: 
20: 3. **接口不可知论**：核心域逻辑保持纯净且独立于接口问题，通过端口和适配器模式同时支持自动化优先的 CLI 工作流和交互式 GUI 体验。
21: 
22: ### 1.2 核心架构模式
23: 
24: #### 六边形架构（端口和适配器）
25: 系统实现了严格的**六边形架构**，以 `cowork-core` crate 为中心，包含纯域逻辑。所有外部关注点（LLM API、文件系统、用户界面）通过明确定义的端口连接：
26: 
27: - **入站端口**：`InteractiveBackend` 特性实现 CLI 和 GUI
28: - **出站端口**：用于持久化的仓库抽象、用于 AI 集成的 LLM 客户端接口
29: - **适配器**：`cowork-cli`、`cowork-gui` 和基础设施模块中的具体实现
30: 
31: #### 领域驱动设计（DDD）
32: 架构遵循 DDD 战术模式：
33: - **聚合**：`Project`（根）、`Iteration`、`ProjectMemory` 实施一致性边界
34: - **值对象**：`Artifacts`、`StageResult`、`InheritanceMode`（完整/部分/无）
35: - **域服务**：流程编排、继承分析、变更范围检测
36: - **仓库**：`ProjectStore`、`IterationStore`、`MemoryStore` 抽象持久化
37: 
38: #### 事件驱动架构（GUI 层）
39: 基于 Tauri 的桌面应用程序实现事件驱动架构：
40: - **非对称通信**：请求用命令（invoke），流式响应用事件（emit）
41: - **实时流式传输**：LLM 令牌流、进程日志和智能体活动通过 Tauri 事件系统流动
42: - **状态同步**：React 前端维护本地状态，而后端发射状态变更事件
43: 
44: #### Actor-Critic 模式
45: 每个流程阶段实现 Actor-Critic 模式：
46: - **Actor**：根据指令生成工件（代码、文档、计划）
47: - **Critic**：验证质量、检查约束、建议改进
48: - **反馈循环**：人类输入通过批评上下文重新生成 Actor 输出
49: 
50: ### 1.3 技术栈概览
51: 
52: | 层级 | 技术 | 目的 | 架构角色 |
53: |-------|-----------|---------|-------------------|
54: | **核心域** | Rust + Tokio | 异步运行时执行流程 | 域逻辑隔离 |
55: | **AI 编排** | adk-rust | 智能体框架和工具生态系统 | AI 智能体生命周期管理 |
56: | **LLM 集成** | OpenAI 兼容 API | 代码生成和推理 | 基础设施适配器 |
57: | **限流** | 自定义信号量 + 延迟 | 30 次/分钟合规 | 横切关注点 |
58: | **CLI 接口** | clap + dialoguer | 命令解析和终端 UI | 主要适配器 |
59: | **GUI 后端** | Tauri | 桌面运行时和系统集成 | 次要适配器 |
60: | **GUI 前端** | React 18 + Ant Design | 组件化交互式 UI | 表示层 |
61: | **持久化** | JSON + serde | 模式演化和存储 | 仓库实现 |
62: | **安全** | 路径验证 + 沙箱 | 工作区隔离 | 安全边界 |
63: 
64: ---
65: 
66: ## 2. 系统上下文（C4 1 级）
67: 
68: ### 2.1 系统定位和价值
69: 
70: Cowork Forge 定位为**本地优先 AI 开发环境**，介于传统 IDE 和基于云的 AI 编码助手之间。与云解决方案不同，它保持完整的数据 locality，同时提供简单代码补全工具无法实现的结构化 AI 编排。
71: 
72: **核心价值主张**：
73: - **连续性**：内存系统跨开发会话保留架构决策
74: - **一致性**：强制的 7 阶段流程确保系统化开发方法论
75: - **控制**：人在回路门在关键阶段防止 AI 幻觉传播
76: - **灵活性**：双接口支持同时满足自动化脚本和探索性开发
77: 
78: ### 2.2 用户角色和场景
79: 
80: ```mermaid
81: flowchart TB
82:     subgraph Users["用户生态系统"]
83:         ID["个人开发者<br/>自动化导向"]
84:         DT["开发团队<br/>标准化导向"]
85:         AD["AI 增强开发者<br/>探索导向"]
86:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
87:     end
88:     
89:     subgraph Value["价值交付"]
90:         RP["快速原型设计<br/>从想法到代码只需数分钟"]
91:         KM["知识管理<br/>跨迭代记忆"]
92:         QC["质量控制<br/>Actor-Critic 验证"]
93:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
94:     end
95:     
96:     ID -->|CLI 自动化| RP
97:     DT -->|标准化工作流| KM
98:     AD -->|交互式 GUI| QC
99: ```
100: 
101: **主要用户原型**：
102: 
103: 1. **个人开发者**：利用 CLI 进行快速原型设计，通过自动化从自然语言描述生成样板和脚手架。
104: 
105: 2. **开发团队**：使用内存系统跨多个项目维护架构标准，使用继承模式系统地演进现有代码库。
106: 
107: 3. **AI 增强开发者**：更喜欢 GUI 接口以了解 AI 决策过程，利用实时流式传输和 HITL 验证以交互方式引导开发过程。
108: 
109: ### 2.3 外部系统交互
110: 
111: ```mermaid
112: flowchart TB
113:     subgraph CoworkForge["Cowork Forge 系统边界"]
114:         Core["核心域引擎<br/>(Rust)"]
115:         CLI["CLI 接口"]
116:         GUI["桌面 GUI<br/>(Tauri + React)"]
117:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
118:     end
119:     
120:     subgraph External["外部系统"]
121:         LLM["LLM 提供商 API<br/>(OpenAI 兼容)"]
122:         FS["本地文件系统"]
123:         Shell["Shell/命令执行器"]
124:         Editor["外部编辑器<br/>(系统默认)"]
125:         DevServer["开发服务器<br/>(Vite 等)"]
126:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
127:     end
128:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
129:     
130:     User["开发者/用户"] -->|命令| CLI
131:     User -->|交互| GUI
132:     GUI <-->|事件/命令| Core
133:     CLI -->|调用| Core
134:     
135:     Core -->|API 调用<br/>限流: 30 次/分钟| LLM
136:     Core -->|验证的 I/O| FS
137:     Core -->|进程生成| Shell
138:     Core -->|编辑调用| Editor
139:     Core -->|进程管理| DevServer
140:     Core -->|远程工具集成| MCP
141: ```
142: 
143: **外部依赖**：
144: 
145: - **LLM 提供商 API**：OpenAI 兼容端点用于智能体推理。交互限流（每分钟 30 次请求）并带有并发控制（单一信号量）以管理 API 配额和成本。
146: 
147: - **本地文件系统**：项目、迭代和内存的主要持久化机制。访问通过工作区验证约束以防止路径遍历攻击。
148: 
149: - **Shell/命令执行器**：用于项目验证（依赖安装、构建、测试）和开发服务器管理。命令在项目工作区边界内进行清理和执行。
150: 
151: - **外部编辑器**：在 HITL 流程中调用的系统默认编辑器，用于内容审查和修改（如 vim、VS Code、nano）。
152: 
153: - **开发服务器**：用户提供的服务器进程（Vite、Webpack 开发服务器），通过 ProcessRunner 管理以提供实时预览功能。
154: 
155: 
156: - **MCP 服务器**：提供外部 AI 能力，通过 Model Context Protocol 连接（如 Tavily 网络搜索、DeepWiki 代码文档查询）。配置通过 `config.toml` 文件的 `[mcp]` 部分管理，支持应用启动时自动初始化并注入工具到所有 Agents。
157: ### 2.4 系统边界定义
158: 
159: **范围内组件**：
160: - 核心域逻辑（迭代、项目、内存聚合）
161: - 带阶段执行器的 7 阶段 AI 智能体流程
162: - 智能体指令库（约 2000 行提示工程）
163: - 工具生态系统（40+ ADK 工具：文件、数据、验证、HITL、内存、部署、Legacy 分析），支持 MCP 远程工具集成（Tavily、DeepWiki 等）
164: - 基于 JSON 的项目/迭代存储的持久化层
165: - 带参数解析的 CLI 命令接口
166: - 基于 Tauri 的 GUI 和 React 前端
167: - 用于开发服务器的实时进程运行器
168: - 限流 LLM 客户端工厂
169: - 跨平台桌面应用程序外壳
170: 
171: **范围外组件**：
172: - 第三方 LLM 训练基础设施
173: - 版本控制系统集成（Git 操作）
174: - 外部包注册表管理（npm、crates.io）
175: - 云部署平台和 CI/CD 流程
176: - 远程协作功能（实时多用户编辑）
177: 
178: ---
179: 
180: ## 3. 容器视图（C4 2 级）
181: 
182: ### 3.1 域模块划分
183: 
184: Cowork Forge 结构为**多 crate Rust 工作区**，具有遵循 DDD 战略设计的清晰域边界：
185: 
186: ```mermaid
187: flowchart TB
188:     subgraph Workspace["Cowork Forge 工作区"]
189:         subgraph Presentation["表现层"]
190:             CLI["cowork-cli<br/>命令路由"]
191:             GUI["cowork-gui<br/>Tauri + React"]
192:         end
193:         
194:         subgraph Application["应用层"]
195:             CLIBackend["CLI 后端<br/>InteractiveBackend 实现"]
196:             GUIBackend["GUI 后端<br/>Tauri 命令"]
197:             ProjectRunner["项目运行器<br/>进程管理"]
198:         end
199:         
200:         subgraph Domain["域层 (cowork-core)"]
201:             subgraph CoreDomains["核心业务域"]
202:                 ProjectDomain["项目域<br/>聚合根"]
203:                 IterationDomain["迭代域<br/>生命周期管理"]
204:                 PipelineDomain["流程域<br/>7 阶段编排"]
205:                 MemoryDomain["内存域<br/>知识管理"]
206:             end
207:             
208:             subgraph SupportingDomains["支撑域"]
209:                 ToolsDomain["工具域<br/>30+ ADK 工具"]
210:                 InteractionDomain["交互域<br/>后端抽象"]
211:             end
212:             
213:             subgraph Infrastructure["基础设施层"]
214:                 Persistence["持久化<br/>JSON 存储"]
215:                 LLMIntegration["LLM 集成<br/>限流客户端"]
216:                 Security["安全<br/>路径验证"]
217:             end
218:         end
219:         MCP["MCP 服务器<br/>(Tavily/DeepWiki)"]
220:     end
221:     
222:     CLI -->|实现| CLIBackend
223:     GUI -->|调用| GUIBackend
224:     CLIBackend -->|使用| InteractionDomain
